resume:
Marcus Reilly — Manufacturing / Process Engineer

Experience
Kessler Components (2021–present) — Process Engineer II
- Owns continuous improvement on a CNC machining line (aluminum housings).
- Ran kaizen events; worked on cycle-time and scrap reduction.
- Coordinated with maintenance on a few line-changeover projects.

Apex Tooling (2018–2021) — Manufacturing Engineer
- Supported tooling and fixture design for a stamping cell.
- Helped roll out 5S across two shifts.

Education
BS, Mechanical Engineering (2018)

Skills
Lean, Six Sigma (Green Belt), SPC, CAD (SolidWorks), root-cause analysis, PFMEA
jd: We are hiring a mid-level Process Engineer for a high-mix CNC machining plant, reporting to the Manufacturing Engineering Manager. You will own throughput and quality on two machining cells, lead structured problem-solving (kaizen, A3, root-cause), and drive measurable improvements in cycle time, scrap, and OEE. Requirements: you can walk an interviewer through one specific improvement you personally led — the baseline number, what you changed, and the result. We expect concrete data (cycle-time seconds, scrap PPM, OEE points) and a clear account of your individual contribution versus the team's. Vague 'we improved efficiency' answers without specifics will be probed hard.

history.q: Tell me about one specific continuous-improvement project you personally led on your CNC line — what was the baseline, what did you change, and what was the measurable result?

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, so we did a bunch of continuous improvement work on the line and it really helped. We ran some kaizen events, looked at the process, and made things more efficient overall. The team did a great job and management was happy with where we ended up. I don't have the exact numbers in front of me, but it was a solid improvement and the line definitely ran better afterward.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: made things more efficient overall

expected-yield: force one concrete before/after number on a single named project (cycle-time seconds, scrap PPM, or OEE points) plus the specific change the candidate personally made, exposing whether any real improvement exists behind the vague framing
